1:
With Benjamin the Chronicler returns, more fully than before, to a tribe he had already touched, and the witnesses ask first why this one house should be traced with such care. The reformed John Gill answers that the genealogy of Benjamin is reviewed because it clung to Judah in the worship of God, went with it into captivity and returned with it out of exile; but chiefly, he says, the review is made for the sake of Saul and his posterity, the first king of Israel, who sprang from this tribe and in whose line the whole register will end. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch find the manner of the list remarkable: Benjamin’s sons are numbered “Bela his firstborn, Ashbel the second, Aharah the third,” where Gen. 46:21 had set Becher second and Ashbel third, and where the names Nohah and Rapha are found nowhere else among Benjamin’s sons. Aharah, they urge, is plainly the Ahiram of Num. 26:38, so that only those sons are named here who founded the families of the tribe, Nohah and Rapha answering to the Shephupham and Hupham of Num. 26:39.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry confesses there is little history in these verses and much difficulty, and counsels the reader not to perplex himself over it. Ezra, he supposes, copied the lists as he found them in the books of the kings of Israel and Judah, each tribe having given in its own after its own fashion; some ascend and some descend, some are fuller and some shorter, some agree with other records and some differ, and those of Dan and Reuben were lost altogether. The holy man wrote as he was moved by the Holy Ghost, yet was under no necessity to mend the defects or rectify the mistakes of the genealogies; it was enough that he set down so much as served the present purpose, the directing of the returned captives to settle again with their own families. And Henry lifts the dry list into praise: many mighty nations of that age lie buried in oblivion, while the names of the Israel of God are here kept in everlasting remembrance, a figure of the writing of names in the Lamb’s book of life. He remembers too that this very tribe had once been cut down in the matter of Gibeah until but six hundred men escaped, and yet makes as good a figure here as any, for it is God’s honour to raise up the diminished and abased. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the plan of the chapter plainly: the genealogy of Benjamin runs down to Saul through the first two-and-thirty verses, and then the children and descendants of Saul fill out its close, and he refers the reader back to what he had written at ch. 7:6.
2:
The count of Benjamin’s founding sons is completed with Nohah the fourth and Rapha the fifth, and the witnesses use the two odd names to explain the whole method of the list. The reformed John Gill reports the older guesses: Nohah is supposed by some to be the same person as Becher, by others as Naaman, and Rapha the same as the Rosh who stands in the parallel roll — one name serving, as often in these registers, for a house known elsewhere by another. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press deeper by turning to Bela’s own sons, Addar, Gera, and Abihud. Of the six names borne by them, Gera is met with twice, and Naaman appears in Gen. 46:21 as a son of Benjamin but in Num. 26:40 as a grandson; Shephuphan is but another form of Shephupham, and Huram may be a copyist’s slip for Hupham, just as Addar probably stands for the Ard of the older text.

The recurrence of one name in two places, Keil and Delitzsch argue, is incomprehensible only if the word “sons” be pressed in its narrowest sense; but if it means descendants who founded fathers’-houses, then two cousins might well bear a single name, and Addar, Shephuphan, and Huram may be different men from the Ard, Shephupham, and Hupham of the law. This is the governing key to the whole chapter: these are not a bare string of individuals but the heads of clans, and the same name may sit in two branches without contradiction. So the witnesses turn what looks at first like a tangle of disagreeing lists into a coherent record of the families of Benjamin, each name a house and not merely a man, and the differences from Num. 26:38 the ordinary marks of registers kept by different hands for different ends. Gill’s scattered identifications and Keil’s patient philology meet in the same conviction, that nothing here contradicts the older books once the abridged and clan-based character of the record is understood. And the very divergences that trouble a first reader — Becher missing here, Nohah and Rapha standing where the law had set Shephupham and Hupham — become, on this reading, not errors at all but the mark of a historian naming the surviving houses of his own day, the branches that still stood when the tribe came up from exile, rather than mechanically recopying the twelve sons of the patriarch. The list, in short, is a census of clans and not a birth-roll of children, and read so it falls at once into agreement with the older books.
3:
Bela, Benjamin’s firstborn, is now opened into his own sons, Addar, Gera, and Abihud, and the witnesses treat the short list as a lesson in reading these names rightly. The reformed John Gill takes up the first of them, Addar, and judges him the same as the Ard who stands among the sons of Benjamin in the older record, though here he is in truth a grandson; and Gera likewise, he notes, is set down where an incautious eye might take him for a son when he too belongs to the later generation. The point is one Gill presses throughout: the abridged register slides from father to grandson without always marking the step, so that a man called a “son” of Benjamin in one place is a grandson in another, and the reader must supply the missing links.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose account of Bela’s house governs this stretch, make the same elasticity a settled principle rather than a difficulty. Because Gera appears twice among these names, and because the word rendered “sons” reaches beyond immediate children to the founders of related households, they conclude that Addar, Shephuphan, and Huram may be distinct persons from the Ard, Shephupham, and Hupham of Num. 26:39, cousins bearing inherited names. Abihud, they add, is met with as a descendant of Benjamin only here, and the name Ahoah that follows may be connected with the Ahiah of v.7. The witnesses thus leave the verse not as a contradiction to be explained away but as a specimen of how the Chronicler works: he compresses generations, keeps the founders of houses, and lets a name recur where a clan has carried it, trusting his first readers to know from the law and their own records how the links were joined that he has left unspoken. The difficulty is felt most keenly, the witnesses imply, by the reader who forgets that a genealogy of clans is not a register of individuals; once that is granted, the doubled Gera and the shifting between Ard and Addar cease to trouble at all. Gill’s willingness to call a man a grandson where the older text called him a son, and Keil’s principle that inherited names travelled from father to cousin, work to the same end, leaving the three sons of Bela as a faithful roster of the households that traced themselves back to Benjamin’s firstborn.
4:
The sons of Bela are carried on — Abishua, Naaman, Ahoah, Gera, Shephuphan, and Huram — and the commentators sort them against the older lists name by name. The reformed John Gill takes them all as sons of Bela: Naaman, he notes, is reckoned among the very sons of Benjamin, and the family of the Naamanites is named from this grandson; Ahoah some think the same as the Ehi of the older record; and Shephuphan and Huram seem to be the Shupham and Hupham known elsewhere. He records too the alternative that the last three might be sons of Ehud, named in the following verse, though he does not press it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch turn from the names to the history folded around them, for the sons of Ehud, whose descent from the men of these verses is not given, are heads of fathers’-houses of the inhabitants of Geba — Geba of Benjamin, the Levite town of 1 Chron. 6:45, mentioned in 1 Sam. 13:16 and marked in Josh. 18:24, surviving still, they say, as the half-ruinous village Jeba some three leagues north of Jerusalem.

From this settled town, Keil and Delitzsch continue, a company was carried captive to Manahath, namely Naaman and Ahiah and Gera, and it was Gera who was the author of their removal — a knot of clan-history that the next verses will draw tighter. The witnesses thus read the roll of Bela’s sons as both a genealogy and the seed of a story: behind the bare names lie real houses seated at Geba, some of them uprooted and driven off by their own kinsmen. Gill’s careful matching of Naaman and Ahoah and Shephuphan to their counterparts in the law keeps the record tied to the earlier books, while Keil’s note on Geba and Manahath shows that the Chronicler is not merely counting men but tracing the movements of the tribe, the towns it held and the feuds by which its clans displaced one another. Beneath the placid surface of a name-list, that is, lies the turbulence of a living tribe — households rooted at Geba, kinsmen carried off to Manahath, a leading clan named Gera setting the whole removal in motion — and the witnesses read the six sons of Bela as the doorway into that half-lost history rather than as a mere tally of men. Gill keeps the reader tied to the law by his patient matching of the names, and Keil opens beneath them the drama of a people on the move, so that the verse is at once a pedigree and the first line of a story the following verses will tell.
6:
“And these are the sons of Ehud” — and at once the witnesses divide over the identity of the man. The reformed John Gill insists he is not the famous judge, but perhaps a son of the Huram just named, and warns that the three names following are to be read as a parenthesis, the sons meant being those set down in the next verse. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had leaned the other way in his opening survey, taking the Ehud here, with the Gera nearby and the man who begat children in Moab, to be that Ehud who was the second of the judges, the son of Gera and a Benjamite of Judg. 3:15, whose slaying of the king of Moab might well have given his posterity a footing in that land. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott holds the question open: the difference in the Hebrew between this Ehud and Ehud the judge is so slight that an original identity may perhaps be assumed, though he offers as an alternative that the name may be one with the Abihud of v.3, comparing such pairs as Nadab and Abinadab.

The rest of the verse, Ellicott grants, is unusually obscure, its historical allusions no longer certainly explicable; most interpreters assume a parenthesis reaching from “these are the sons of Ehud” down to “he removed them” in the next verse, so that Uzza and Ahihud are the sons intended. “These are the heads of the fathers” he renders heads of father-houses, groups of kindred clans; and “removed them” he corrects to “carried them captive” or transported, the very word used of the Assyrian deportation in 1 Chron. 5:26. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the plural “these” of the men named next, whom the historian describes by their places of dwelling rather than by an exact account of their parents, because the genealogies of Benjamin had been broken by the almost total extirpation of the tribe recorded in Judg. 20:29-48. So the witnesses leave a verse whose grammar is knotted and whose Ehud is disputed, agreeing only that the names before them are clan-heads of Geba, some of whom were about to be driven from their homes.
7:
The knot left hanging in the previous verse is now tied: “and Naaman, and Ahiah, and Gera, he removed them, and begat Uzza, and Ahihud.” The reformed John Gill reads the opening words as resuming the interrupted list, the three names to be joined to what went before. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest account, rendering the tangled clause thus: these are the sons of Ehud — heads of clans of the inhabitants of Geba, and men carried them away to Manahath, both Naaman and Ahiah and Gera, he it was who carried them away — he begat Uzza and Ahihud. On this reading Uzza and Ahihud, two chiefs of clans settled at Geba, were forcibly removed to Manahath, the place named in 1 Chron. 2:52 and 2:54, by three other Benjamite clans, of which Gera was the leading one; and he suggests the phrase might even be read “for the sake of peace,” a hint of the feuds that drove clan against clan within the tribe.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the thread forward to Shaharaim, whose descent from the sons already named is obscure, and whose family fills the next verses. He was the father of a considerable number of heads of fathers’-houses, born to him of two or three wives; and, as the text will say, he begat children in the country of Moab after he had put certain of them away. The witnesses find in this crowded, difficult verse a picture of Benjamin in motion: clans seated at Geba, others carrying them off to Manahath, and a man like Shaharaim driven at length as far as Moab. Ellicott and Keil agree that the very obscurity is the mark of a genuine record, its historical allusions once plain to those who kept it and now only half-recoverable; the Chronicler has preserved, in these broken notices, the real convulsions of a tribe that had never fully healed from the wound of Gibeah, its households displaced and scattered even into the land of Moab across the Jordan. What the older narratives had told as war and exile the Chronicler compresses into a handful of proper names and a single verb of removal, trusting his readers to hear the whole history behind them; and the witnesses, refusing to force the obscurity, are content to mark where the record grows dark and to follow the one thread it keeps clear. That thread is Shaharaim, whose passage into Moab will occupy the next verses, and whom Ellicott and Keil already have in view even as they finish untangling the removal to Manahath that this verse relates.
8:
The scene shifts across the Jordan: “Shaharaim begat children in the country of Moab, after he had sent them away.” The reformed John Gill offers only a link in the chain, taking Shaharaim for a son of Ahihud, or rather a brother of his and so another son of Ehud, keeping the man within the house already traced. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confesses that Shaharaim seems out of all connection with the other Benjamite houses; some would identify him with the Ahishahar of 1 Chron. 7:10 because the names are near in meaning, and some even with the mysterious Aher of 1 Chron. 7:12, but he judges it simpler to suppose that the words “and Shaharaim” have dropped from the end of the preceding verse. Expelled from Geba with the other clans, Shaharaim found a refuge in Moab, as David’s parents would later do in 1 Sam. 22:3-4 and as the family of Elimelech had done in the days of Ruth 1.

Ellicott presses on into the corrupt Hebrew of the verse. The easiest correction, he says, yields the sense “Shaharaim begat in the country of Moab, after divorcing Hushim and Baara his wives, of Hodesh his wife Jobab,” and this the Septuagint supports. And he reads the whole in the figurative style of these genealogies, where the amalgamation of clans is spoken of as a marriage and the emigration of a clan from its old home as a divorce: Shaharaim’s branching in Moab into seven new households is told as the fruit of putting away his first wives and taking another. Hushim, he adds, is itself a Benjamite clan named in 1 Chron. 7:12. The witnesses thus read the verse as the record of a scattered house planting itself afresh in a foreign land, its clan-movements clothed in the language of marriage and divorce, so that a genealogy becomes the coded memory of a people’s wanderings and resettlements beyond its ancient borders. Gill’s quieter reading, keeping Shaharaim among the sons of Ehud, and Ellicott’s bolder reconstruction of the mangled Hebrew agree at least in this, that a Benjamite house had been uprooted from Geba and had struck new root in Moab. And the very fact that the register preserves so foreign a chapter of the tribe’s life shows how careful the Chronicler was to lose no branch, even one that had wandered clean out of the land, but to gather it back into the roll of Benjamin as though the exile itself were part of the family’s story.
9:
In Moab Shaharaim raises a new family, and the witnesses gather about the name of the wife who bore it. “He begat of Hodesh his wife Jobab, and Zibia, and Mesha, and Malcham,” and the reformed John Gill notes that these, with three more, are the seven sons begotten of her; but he rejects the Targum’s notion that Hodesh was only another name for Baara, “called so because she was newly espoused” — that, he says plainly, is wrong. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes exactly the opposite view, following the Targum that renders “he begot of Baara, that is Chodesh, his wife,” and reasoning that since the earlier verse named Hushim and Baara as the wives while this names Hodesh, Hodesh must be Baara under a new name, given her because Shaharaim had put her away and now espoused her afresh. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott stands nearer to Gill: Hodesh he takes for the new Moabite wife, or settlement, of Shaharaim, distinct from the wives left behind.

Ellicott adds a telling observation about the sons themselves, that two of them bear a Moabite cast: Mesha recalls Mesha king of Moab in 2 Kings 3:4, and Malcham echoes Malcham, or Milcom, the god of Moab and Ammon named in Jer. 49:1. The names of the children, that is, carry the mark of the land where they were born, the very flavour of Moab clinging to a Benjamite house transplanted there. So the witnesses read the verse both as a small textual dispute — whether Hodesh be Baara renamed or a fresh wife — and as a quiet witness to how a family took the colour of its exile, its sons christened with the names of Moab’s kings and gods even while they remained, by descent, sons of Benjamin. The disagreement over Hodesh is honestly left standing; Clarke with the Targum makes her Baara restored, Gill and Ellicott make her another woman, and the text does not compel a verdict. What none of the witnesses doubts is that the seven sons named here became so many heads of fathers’-houses in the land of Moab, a whole cluster of clans sprung from one exiled Benjamite; and the small quarrel over Hodesh’s name only sharpens the larger wonder they all feel, that a tribe’s genealogy should run for a season through a foreign country. That a Mesha and a Malcham should be born to a son of Benjamin, wearing the very names of Moab’s king and Moab’s god, is for the witnesses the plainest sign of how deeply the exile had marked the house, and how faithfully the Chronicler recorded even that.
10:
The roll of Shaharaim’s Moabite sons closes and a second branch opens: “of Hushim he begat Abitub, and Elpaal.” The reformed John Gill fixes the timing with a single stroke, noting that these two were begotten of Hushim before Shaharaim sent her away, or divorced her — the offspring, that is, of the marriage that preceded the new household in Moab. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the phrase “heads of the fathers” that has just gathered up the sons of Hodesh, referring the reader back to his note at v.6, where he had explained it as heads of father-houses, the chiefs of kindred clans rather than single men.

Brief as the notice is, the witnesses let it complete the double picture of Shaharaim’s house. By one wife, Hodesh, he had the seven clans that took root in Moab; by another, Hushim, whom he afterward put away, he had Abitub and Elpaal, and it is Elpaal, as the following verses will show, who becomes the fruitful stem from which a whole cluster of Benjamite fathers’-houses descends. Gill’s small chronological gloss — “before he divorced her” — is more than pedantry, for it distinguishes the elder line, sprung from the marriage left behind in Geba or its neighbourhood, from the younger clans of the Moabite settlement; and Ellicott’s insistence that “heads of the fathers” means the chiefs of clans keeps the reader from mistaking these names for a mere family tree of individuals. The verse thus stands as a hinge: it looks back to Hushim and the divorce, and forward to Elpaal, whose sons and grandsons will spread through Ono and Lod and Aijalon and at last dwell in Jerusalem itself. What might pass for a bare pair of names is in truth the trunk from which the largest branch of this genealogy is about to grow. The witnesses’ care over so slight a verse is therefore not misplaced: to distinguish the sons of Hushim from the sons of Hodesh, and the marriage kept from the marriage put away, is to keep straight the whole descent that follows, in which it is Elpaal’s house, and not the Moabite clans, that will carry the story of Benjamin down toward Aijalon and Jerusalem. Gill’s single word about the timing and Ellicott’s cross-reference to his earlier note thus do more work than their brevity suggests, sorting the elder line from the younger at the very point where the two threads part.
12:
Elpaal’s house now spreads, and one of his sons is credited with the founding of towns: “Eber, and Misham, and Shamed, who built Ono, and Lod, with the towns thereof.” The reformed John Gill simply adds these to Elpaal’s sons already named. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the builder and the places: the name, he says, is really Shemer, the same that stood as an Asherite clan in 1 Chron. 7:34, and the clause is a parenthesis, “he built Ono and Lod and her daughters.” Ono, now Kefr Auna, recurs among the returning exiles in Ezra 2:33 and Neh. 11:35 and is always coupled with Lod; and Lod itself is the Lydda of Acts 9:32, still standing as the village of Ludd, north of Ramleh on the road between Jaffa and Jerusalem.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws in the Targum’s dark footnote to these towns, that Ono and Lod were the cities “which the children of Israel ravaged and burnt with fire when they made war on the tribe of Benjamin in Gibeah” — the paraphrast reaching back to the civil war of the judges, when Benjamin was all but destroyed, and reading the building of these towns as their rebuilding after that ruin. The witnesses thus give the verse a double depth. On the surface it is a note of clan settlement, Elpaal’s son planting or rebuilding two towns of the lowland; beneath it lie both the long after-life of those towns, named again among the villages of the Return, and the old memory of Gibeah, when the tribe had been reduced almost to nothing. Ellicott’s geography carries the names forward to the days of Ezra and even of the apostles, while Clarke’s Targum carries them back to the darkest hour of Benjamin’s history, so that Ono and Lod stand as monuments both of a tribe’s near-extinction and of its stubborn recovery in the land. The witnesses do not press the Targum’s tale as certain, but they let it colour the reading, for it accords with all they know of Benjamin — a tribe all but wiped out and yet rebuilding its towns and multiplying its clans. And Ellicott’s sober geography, tracing Ono and Lod through the lists of Ezra and Nehemiah down to the Lydda of the apostles, shows those same towns still standing centuries later, long outliving the men who first built them, so that a single parenthesis about two lowland villages reaches from the war of Gibeah to the age of the church.
13:
Two more of Elpaal’s house are named with a deed attached: “Beriah also, and Shema, who were heads of the fathers of the inhabitants of Aijalon, who drove away the inhabitants of Gath.” The reformed John Gill takes Beriah and Shema for sons of Elpaal. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the grammar and the exploit: after these two names the Masoretes set a stop, so that either they close the sons of Elpaal or, disconnected from the foregoing, they begin a fresh series of Benjamite clans. He renders the clause with an emphatic pronoun twice over — “they were heads of the clans of the inhabitants of Aijalon; they put to flight the inhabitants of Gath” — and reads it of the clans of Beriah and Shema, settled at Aijalon, the modern Yalo near Gibeon, who expelled a Gittite population and dwelt in their stead. There is plain allusion, he says, to some famous feat, in which these two Benjamite houses fared better than the Ephraimites Ezer and Elead of 1 Chron. 7:21; and he cautions against confusing this Beriah with the Ephraimite Beriah of 1 Chron. 7:23 or the Asherite of 1 Chron. 7:30.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, in his survey of the chapter, had already caught the same exploit and given it a moral edge: here, he thought, were Benjamites who drove away the men of Gath, perhaps the very people, or the posterity of the people, who had slain the sons of Ephraim in 1 Chron. 7:21, so that the deed was a kind of reprisal — and one of those who did this piece of justice bore the name Beriah, the name that itself preserved the memory of that earlier injury. The witnesses thus read the verse as the record of a victory that answered an old defeat: where Ephraim had lost sons to the men of Gath, Benjamin regained ground from them, driving the Gittites out of Aijalon. Ellicott’s careful separation of the several Beriahs keeps the exploit from being muddled with other houses of the same name, and Henry’s reflection lifts it into providence, the wrong done to one tribe made good, generations later, by the courage of another.
14:
The house of Elpaal keeps branching — “Ahio, Shashak, and Jerimoth” — and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the run to lay down a caution against over-cleverness. Several of these names recur elsewhere in Scripture, yet nowhere else are they met with as sons of Beriah or of Elpaal, and the scholar Bertheau, they report, would identify three of Elpaal’s sons — Meshullam, Heber, and Ishmerai — with the Misham, Eber, and Shemer of v.12. But this Keil and Delitzsch refuse, and for good reason: it is questionable whether the Elpaal whose sons are named in these verses is even the same person as the Elpaal of the earlier verse, so that to fuse the names is to build on sand. The reformed John Gill, for his part, simply enrolls Ahio, Shashak, and Jerimoth among the sons of Elpaal, content to receive the list as it stands.

What Keil and Delitzsch confess about the whole company is disarmingly plain: of these descendants of Elpaal nothing further is known, and the same must be said of the sons of Shimhi, of Shashak, and of Jeroham who follow. The concluding remark that will come at v.28, “these are heads of fathers’-houses,” reaches back over all of them, from this verse to the end of the roll. The witnesses thus read the passage as a register of clan-chiefs whose names alone survive, their deeds and histories long since lost; and they take the loss soberly, neither inventing stories to fill the silence nor pressing doubtful identifications to make the names seem to say more than they do. It is the discipline of the whole chapter in little: the record is received as it is, its founders of houses numbered and honoured, and the temptation to knit every repeated name into one is set firmly aside. Several of these names — a Michael, a Meshullam, a Zebadiah — are indeed met with elsewhere in Scripture, but singly, and never as sons of this Beriah or this Elpaal, so that their recurrence is no warrant for fusing houses that the text keeps apart. The witnesses find in Keil’s restraint a model for reading the whole chapter: where the material is thin, the honest course is to register what stands and to resist the scholar’s itch to tidy the record by conjecture, since a genealogy so crowded with common names will mislead anyone who takes resemblance for proof.
15:
The list runs on through Zebadiah, Arad, and Ader, and the reformed John Gill supplies the thread that ties them in: all that follow, in this verse and the next, were the sons of Beriah the son of Elpaal. It is a small clarifying word, but it matters, for without it the reader might lose the line of descent among so many bare names; Gill keeps the branches sorted, marking where Beriah’s sons begin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, handling the same block, count six sons of Beriah across these verses and observe again that, although several of the names occur elsewhere in Scripture singly, nowhere else are they found as Beriah’s sons — the register stands alone in preserving them.

The witnesses make of this the occasion for a quiet reflection that the chapter has been pressing from the first. Here are men of whom nothing is told but their names and their father, chiefs of Benjamite households whose whole recorded history is that they existed and belonged to Beriah. Yet the Chronicler set them down, and the commentators do not despise them for their obscurity. Matthew Henry’s word over the opening of the chapter hovers here too: such men, he says, are the Jasher and the Jeshurun, the upright ones, and the memory of the just is blessed. Zebadiah and Arad and Ader are nobodies to later history and yet somebodies to the record of the covenant people, chiefs of households in Israel whose one surviving distinction is that they were reckoned among the sons of Beriah. Gill’s patient sorting of them under their father, together with Keil’s honest note that they are known from nowhere else, leaves the verse as a testimony that in the reckoning of God a man’s obscurity to the ages is no bar to his being remembered by name; and that the least-known head of the least-known clan has, in this bare register, an honour that the forgotten conquerors of forgotten empires never won. And there is this too, that the Beriah whose sons these are is the same whose house, with Shema’s, had driven the men of Gath from Aijalon a few verses before; so that Zebadiah and Arad and Ader, obscure as they stand, spring from a clan the record has already crowned with a famous exploit, and inherit a name that Benjamin had good cause to remember.
17:
A fresh knot of names opens — “Zebadiah, and Meshullam, and Hezeki, and Heber” — and the reformed John Gill links them forward with his customary economy, “these, with those that follow,” gathering this verse and the next into a single household. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place them among the sons of Elpaal, and it is precisely here that they meet the tempting resemblance they have already declined to press: Meshullam and Heber sound like the Misham and Eber of the earlier list, and Bertheau would join them, but Keil and Delitzsch hold the names apart, unwilling to build identity on an echo.

The verse invites a reflection on how thickly the same names lie across these registers. A Zebadiah has just been named among the sons of Beriah, and another stands here among the sons of Elpaal; Meshullam and Heber recur; and the reader who did not attend to the fathers named at each turn would soon be lost. The witnesses take this recurrence not as confusion but as the very texture of Hebrew genealogy, where a stock of honoured names passed from house to house and generation to generation, so that the same word might mark a dozen different men. Gill’s brisk connective and Keil’s refusal to fuse the similar names work to the same end, keeping the clans distinct beneath their shared vocabulary of names. And behind the philology stands the plainer truth the chapter keeps returning to: these are heads of fathers’-houses of Benjamin, chiefs of real households in the tribe, whose names the Chronicler preserved because they were his people, even when he could tell nothing of them but that they lived and led their clans. The summation of v.28 will gather them all as the heads of the fathers who dwelt at last in Jerusalem. The witnesses are content, then, to let this verse do its modest office: it enrolls four more chiefs of a Benjamite house, marks by the recurring names how a stock of family designations passed from branch to branch, and holds the line of descent clear for the summation to come. Where a careless reader sees only a jumble of Zebadiahs and Hebers, Gill and Keil see the ordered households of a real tribe, each set under its own father, and each awaiting the single sentence that will name their common dwelling in the holy city.
19:
A new house begins with “Jakim, and Zichri, and Zabdi,” the first of the sons of Shimhi, and the reformed John Gill marks the opening with a brief connective, “Jakim, with all the rest in these verses,” signalling that a fresh cluster of clan-heads now runs on. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had numbered them in advance: nine sons of Shimhi fill these verses, and of them, as of the neighbouring houses, nothing further is known, though a name here and there is found elsewhere on its own. The register simply preserves them, chiefs of a Benjamite household whose story has otherwise perished.

It is worth pausing, the witnesses suggest, on the eloquence of that silence. Nine men are named as the sons of Shimhi, each of them the head of a family in Israel, and not a single deed of theirs survives; they are known to us only because a genealogist counted them among the heads of the fathers of Benjamin. Yet the very fact of their being written is, in the mind of the chapter, no small thing, for it means that a house which ordinary history would have swallowed without a trace stood, in the reckoning that mattered, among the recorded people of God. Some of these names, Keil and Delitzsch grant, are met with elsewhere in Scripture standing alone, but never here as the sons of Shimhi; this register is their sole memorial. And the sheer number of them — nine heads sprung from a single father — is itself part of the record’s witness, showing a tribe not dwindling but branching, its households multiplying even in the obscurest corner of the roll. Jakim and Zichri and Zabdi have no other monument, but they have this one; and Gill’s quiet gathering of them with Keil’s honest “nothing further is known” leaves the verse as a reminder that God keeps account of families that leave no other mark, and that to be numbered among his people, even namelessly to the later ages, is to be numbered where the numbering counts. And the witnesses remember that these nine, obscure as each name stands, are among the very heads of houses whom the coming summation will place in Jerusalem; the sons of Shimhi, unknown to history, were reckoned among the chief men who held, or would hold, the holy city — a station the record honours above any fame.
22:
Another household opens with “Ishpan, and Heber, and Eliel,” the first of the eleven sons of Shashak, and the reformed John Gill gathers them with a single sweep — “Ishpan, and Heber, and all that follow to the end of these verses” — letting the whole cluster stand together. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had reckoned their number in advance, eleven sons of Shashak filling this stretch of the roll, and had spoken over them the same sober verdict pronounced on all these houses: nothing further is known of them, save that a name or two appears elsewhere on its own.

The witnesses let the sheer length of the list carry its own meaning. Eleven sons to Shashak, nine to Shimhi, six to Jeroham, and before them the crowded houses of Elpaal and Beriah — the tribe that had once been reduced at Gibeah to six hundred men is shown here teeming with clans, its households multiplied beyond counting. Matthew Henry had drawn the lesson at the chapter’s head, that it is the honour of God to help the weakest and to raise up those that are most diminished and abased; and the swarming sons of Shashak are that honour made visible, a nearly-extinguished tribe grown numerous again. Gill’s refusal to linger over each name, sweeping them up together, suits the point: what matters is less the individual Ishpan or Eliel than the fulness of the roll, the evidence of a people restored. The witnesses receive the eleven names as they stand, chiefs of Benjamite households whose deeds are lost but whose multitude testifies that the God who had let the tribe be cut down had also caused it to spring up and fill its towns again. Henry had recalled the low ebb of Benjamin in the days of the judges, when the iniquity of Gibeah left but six hundred men alive of the whole tribe; measured against that near-annihilation, the eleven houses of Shashak, ranked among the many houses of Elpaal and Shimhi and Jeroham, are a standing wonder of recovery. The witnesses do not labour the point, but they let the very length of the roll preach it: a people God had all but blotted out he had multiplied again into a crowd of clans, and the dry list of Shashak’s sons is the plain receipt of that mercy.
26:
The last of these clan-lists begins with “Shamsherai, and Shehariah, and Athaliah,” heads among the six sons of Jeroham, and the reformed John Gill introduces them with his briefest connective yet — “who, with those next mentioned” — knowing the roll is nearly done and needing only to keep the line unbroken. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had counted these six sons of Jeroham as the last of the households named before the summation, and had said of them, as of all the rest, that nothing further is known, though here and there a name recurs elsewhere in Scripture by itself.

With this house the great catalogue of Benjamite clan-heads reaches its end, and the witnesses feel the whole passage bending at last toward its conclusion. Everything from the sons of Beriah onward, Keil and Delitzsch remind us, is about to be gathered under the single heading of v.28: these were heads of the fathers, by their generations, chief men, and these dwelt in Jerusalem. So the sons of Jeroham are not merely one more obscure branch but the last stones laid before the arch is closed. The Chronicler has numbered house after house — Elpaal, Beriah, Shimhi, Shashak, and now Jeroham — not for antiquarian fulness but to set before the returning exiles the roll of families that had once held the holy city, that they might be moved to settle there again. Gill’s hurrying connective and Keil’s patient counting both serve that end, bringing the long list of names to the threshold of its purpose, the repopulating of Jerusalem by the chief houses of Benjamin whose fathers had dwelt there before. The purpose the whole catalogue has been serving is about to be declared — that these families were noted as dwellers in the holy city so that others, at their return from captivity, might be moved to settle there too, in a place few would choose because it was the post of danger. The sons of Jeroham are the last names laid down before that purpose is spoken; and the witnesses feel the register, which has run through house after house with such patience, now gather itself for the single sentence that gives it all its point. Keil and Delitzsch had counted these six as the last of the clans, and Gill had hurried them in with his briefest word, precisely because both knew the summation stood just ahead — the statement that all these were chief men of their generations, and that they dwelt in Jerusalem.
28:
The long roll is now stamped with its heading: “These were heads of the fathers, by their generations, chief men... these dwelt in Jerusalem.” The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it “these were chiefs of clans; according to their birth-rolls, chiefs,” and finds the doubled word pointed: the writer repeats “heads” to warn his readers that the preceding names are not individual members of single families but heads of clans, the very word able to mean the divisions or companies into which a body was parted, as when Gideon split his men into three heads in Judg. 7:16. The summation, he adds, gathers all the names from v.14 to v.27, and sets these Jerusalem-dwelling branches over against the clans seated at Geba and Manahath in v.6, in Moab in v.9, at Lod and Ono in v.12, and at Aijalon in v.13. The reformed John Gill reads it more simply, taking the heads of the fathers to be all the sons of Elpaal and their sons from v.14 onward.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the practical reason the historian troubled to note their dwelling in Jerusalem: it was that others, at their return from captivity, might be induced to settle there too, which few were willing to do, the city being the post of danger. And he lifts the notice, as Henry had, into praise — many great and mighty nations of that age lie in perpetual oblivion, while the names of multitudes of the Israel of God are here carefully preserved, a figure of God’s writing the names of his spiritual Israel in the Lamb’s book of life. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only that the following verses are so tangled in their names that Dr. Kennicott labored hard to restore the true reading, referring the curious to his collation. The witnesses thus read the verse as the keystone of the whole passage: a register of clan-heads, carefully distinguished from the branches settled elsewhere, set down that the sons of those who once held Jerusalem might be drawn to hold it again. Ellicott’s careful listing of the scattered seats — Geba and Manahath, Moab, Lod and Ono, Aijalon, and Gibeon — throws the Jerusalem clans into relief as the heart of the tribe, while Wesley’s note on the perilous honour of dwelling in the city and Clarke’s on the tangled state of the text remind the reader that this keystone verse is at once a theological summit and a textual thicket. The witnesses hold the two together, reading the summation as the point toward which every foregoing name has been tending, and as the hinge on which the chapter turns from the crowd of Benjamite clans to the single house of Saul.
29:
The register turns from the Jerusalem clans to Gibeon, and with Gibeon toward the house of Saul: “at Gibeon dwelt the father of Gibeon, whose wife’s name was Maachah.” The reformed John Gill takes the father of Gibeon for the builder and prince of the city, and names him Jehiel from the parallel record. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm it: the ancestors of Saul dwelt mainly in Gibeon, now El Jib, some two hours north-west of Jerusalem, marked already in Josh. 9:3; the father, that is the lord and possessor of Gibeon, was called Jehiel according to 1 Chron. 9:35, and his wife Maachah, a name common among the women of Israel, as at 1 Chron. 2:48. The plural “they dwelt” is used, they note, because there dwelt at Gibeon not only the father but his wife and his sons.

Keil and Delitzsch mark at once the textual thinness of the list to come: here eight sons are given, but 1 Chron. 9:36 names ten, and the fuller number is the right one, for two names have fallen out of our verse — Ner between Baal and Nadab, and Mikloth at the end — whose descendants are nonetheless mentioned further on. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes the divergence to reflect on Ezra’s method, reporting the rabbinic tale that, finding two copies that differed in these names, he inserted both, unable to discern which was right; and Clarke judges that if Ezra collated his copies and followed the majority, throwing the rest aside, he had but an imperfect notion of true criticism, so that no wonder faults remain. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that the name Jehiel has here been accidentally omitted, and that the plural “dwelt” shows the “father of Gibeon” to be really the original population of the place under a collective name. The witnesses thus open the Saulide genealogy on a double note, the family’s seat fixed at Gibeon and the text’s own gaps honestly confessed. The witnesses feel the genealogy turning here toward its true object. All the crowded clans of the tribe have been numbered; now the register fixes on Gibeon, and on the one household there from which the first king would spring, tracing the ancestors of Saul with a particularity it gave to no other Israelite house. And they take the frank showing of the text’s seams — a name fallen out here, the sons undercounted against the parallel — not as a weakness but as a mark of the record’s honesty, a genealogy that lets its joins be seen and is mended, where it can be, from its own twin in 1 Chron. 9:44 and the verses before it.
30:
The sons of Jehiel are counted — “his firstborn Abdon, and Zur, and Kish, and Baal, and Nadab” — and the witnesses read the list with one eye on its gap and another on one arresting name. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Abdon was Jehiel’s firstborn, the prince of Gibeon, with the other sons following. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott both mark that a name has fallen out: between Baal and Nadab the register of 1 Chron. 9:36 reads Ner, and it must have stood here too, for Ner is the very link by which Saul will be joined to this house.

It is the name Baal that draws Ellicott’s longer comment. Standing here among the sons of a Gibeonite chief, it prepares the reader for the Eshbaal and Merib-baal who will appear in Saul’s own line at v.33 and v.34, names compounded with Baal, “lord.” For there was a time, Ellicott observes from Hosea 2:16, when the title Baal was innocently applied to Jehovah in common speech — “thou shalt call me Ishi, and shalt no more call me Baali” — so that a devout Israelite might bear such a name without taint. Only after the word became fastened to a foreign and idolatrous worship was it discarded, and the synonymous Adon, or Adonai, put in its place. The witnesses thus find in a single name a whole chapter of Israel’s religious history: Baal, once a harmless word for the Lord, later so defiled by idolatry that the very names of Saul’s house would be rewritten to purge it. Gill’s plain enrolment of the sons and the two scholars’ attention to the missing Ner and the ominous Baal together open the last movement of the chapter, the descent of the first king from the chiefs of Gibeon. The witnesses let the two concerns of the verse stand together: a textual gap that the parallel repairs, and a name freighted with the coming history of Saul’s house. That Abdon and Zur and Kish and Baal and Nadab are given as the sons of a Gibeonite chief, with Ner to be restored among them, sets the stage for the king who will descend from this line; and the very presence of Baal among the brothers, innocent now but ominous in hindsight, prepares the reader for the day when the names of Saul’s own children would be rewritten to scour that syllable away. Gill’s plain enrolment of the sons, Keil’s note on the missing Ner, and Ellicott’s meditation on Baal thus converge on the one point that the royal house is at last in view, its line about to be drawn from these chiefs of Gibeon down to Saul and Jonathan.
32:
“And Mikloth begat Shimeah. And these also dwelt with their brethren in Jerusalem, over against them.” The reformed John Gill notes only that Shimeah is the Shimeam of the parallel place. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the equation and explains it: Shimeah and the Shimeam of 1 Chron. 9:38 are the same name, the latter a mimmated or more ancient form keeping the original ending. More important is what the verse tells of the family’s spread. Of all the stock of Gibeon, Ellicott reads, only this one branch — the house of Mikloth and Shimeah — settled in Jerusalem, though when it did so the text does not say; some refer it to the repeopling of the city after the Restoration, when lots were cast to bring a tenth of the people to dwell there, as in Neh. 11:1.

Ellicott renders the difficult close more exactly than the English, “they also, before their brethren, dwelt in Jerusalem with their brethren,” and unfolds its two phrases. “Before their brethren” he takes in the Hebrew sense of direction, where “before” means east and “behind” west, so that this clan of Gibeon dwelt to the east of their fellow-tribesmen; and “with their brethren” means alongside the other Benjamite clans already settled in the city, the very heads of the fathers gathered at v.28. The witnesses thus bind the Saulide branch to the rest of Benjamin in Jerusalem: the house of Gibeon, from which the first king would come, had a footing in the holy city among its kinsmen. Gill’s brief identification and Ellicott’s careful geography together show the Chronicler doing what he does throughout — locating each clan not only in its descent but in its dwelling, so that the map of Benjamin and the line of Saul are traced in the same breath, and the family of the king is set among the returning people who would repossess Jerusalem. Ellicott’s reading of the two little phrases — the branch dwelling “to the east” of its kin, and “with their brethren” in the city — turns a nearly opaque clause into a precise notice of where this Gibeonite house was seated, among the other Benjamite clans gathered in v.28. The witnesses take it as one more instance of the Chronicler’s double bookkeeping, of blood and of place; and they mark that of all the numerous stock of Gibeon it was this single branch, the house of Mikloth and Shimeah, that came to dwell in the holy city — the very line, as the next verse will show, that ran to Saul.
33:
The genealogy reaches its goal: “Ner begat Kish, and Kish begat Saul, and Saul begat Jonathan, and Malchishua, and Abinadab, and Eshbaal.” The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks how singular this notice is — among all the tribal genealogies there is no mention of the kings of Israel after the revolt from David’s house, not Jeroboam’s family nor Baasha’s nor Omri’s nor Jehu’s, for they were idolaters; but the house of Saul, the royal family before David, is given a particular account. The reformed John Gill and the grammatical-historical witnesses then grapple with the pedigree of Kish, and here they genuinely diverge. Gill, following the Targum, holds that Ner and Abiel were one man under two names, so that Kish’s father is called Ner here and Abiel in 1 Sam. 9:1. Henry, comparing 1 Sam. 14:51, makes Kish the son of Ner but the grandson of Abiel, since Ner was Abiel’s son and Abner, Ner’s son, was Saul’s uncle; it is common, he says, in every language to put “son” for grandson and descendant. Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch take a third road: intermediate names have simply been omitted between Ner and Kish, as often in these lists, and the Ner named here may even be a clan rather than an individual, a namesake of the Ner who fathered Abner.

On Saul’s sons the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the parallels: only three of the four sons of 1 Sam. 14:49 are the same who fell with him in the battle of 1 Sam. 31:2; Abinadab there stands as Ishui in the other place, a case of double naming, and the name itself, like Nadab before it, is a title meaning the father — that is, Jehovah — is noble. Most telling are the last two names. Eshbaal, “man of Baal,” is the true name of the son who appears in 2 Sam. 2:8 as Ish-bosheth, “man of shame,” David’s rival king; and Merib-baal, to come in the next verse, is likewise softened to Mephibosheth — for as idols were styled “abominations,” so the very syllable Baal was purged from these honoured names once it had been defiled. Henry closes the reflection where the chapter will: Saul’s line is carried on only through Jonathan, whose posterity alone is preserved and honoured with a place in the sacred record for the sake of his covenant of kindness with David in 1 Sam. 20:15, a line drawn down through some ten generations to the valiant house of Ulam.
34:
The line narrows to Jonathan’s heir: “the son of Jonathan was Meribbaal; and Meribbaal begat Micah.” The reformed John Gill identifies him at once as the Mephibosheth of the books of Samuel, the change being of the same kind by which Jerubbaal became Jerubbesheth. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the reason for the two names in a word: the Israelites so detested Baal, which means “lord,” that they changed it into “bosheth,” which means shame or reproach, wherever it stood in an honoured name. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the identification from 2 Sam. 4:4 and 2 Sam. 9:6, and gloss the altered form, Mephibosheth, as “exterminans idolum,” the one who scatters the idol — the very name turned into a protest against the god it once invoked.

Keil and Delitzsch add the human detail that the books of Samuel supply, that this Meribbaal was lame in both his feet, crippled in the fall of the day his father and grandfather died; and yet from him the line ran on, for he had a son Micah, of whom came a numerous race. They sketch the descent that the rest of the chapter will trace: Micah’s four sons at the next verse, and then, through the last of them, ten further generations carried down to the great-grandsons of Eshek. The witnesses feel the poignancy of the notice. The grandson of a king, lame and dispossessed, kept alive at David’s table for Jonathan’s sake, becomes the slender thread by which the house of Saul is continued at all; and the Chronicler, faithful to the covenant David swore, follows that thread with care where he passed the idolatrous kings of the north in silence. Gill and Clarke and Keil converge on the same double truth: the name Meribbaal preserves the memory of a defiled worship even as it was rewritten to disown it, and the crippled son of Jonathan preserves, against all likelihood, the honoured line of Israel’s first king. Keil and Delitzsch sketch what the rest of the chapter will unfold, that from this Micah came a numerous race, four sons at the next verse and a line carried through ten generations to the great-grandsons of Eshek; so that the thread which seemed likeliest to snap, in a lame and landless man, runs on in fact further than many a prouder house. The witnesses feel the fitness of it: the family of Saul, spared the silence into which the kings of the north were let fall, is preserved for Jonathan’s sake, and preserved through the very son whom David had sheltered at his own table for the covenant’s sake.
35:
The house of Jonathan spreads in the sons of Micah: “Pithon, and Melech, and Tarea, and Ahaz.” The reformed John Gill compares the parallel and notes that the last but one is there called Tahrea, in 1 Chron. 9:41, where Ahaz is left out altogether, though the Authorised Version rightly supplies him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the same variation as a matter of pronunciation: Tarea and Tahrea are one name, the second a harder utterance of the first, just as the Shimeah of an earlier verse softens the Shimeah of the books of Samuel — the sort of slight phonetic shift that runs harmlessly through the parallel registers.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who take the whole remaining genealogy at a stroke, set these sons of Micah at the head of the ten generations that will run to the end of the chapter, and note that the closing subscription, “all these are of the sons of Benjamin,” will refer back over the whole to the superscription at v.1, binding the last descendants of Saul to the first naming of the tribe. The witnesses read the verse as the quiet continuation of a line that Scripture had every reason to let fall — the posterity of a rejected king, kept alive through a lame and landless grandson — and yet that the Chronicler follows generation by generation. Ahaz here, whose name a copyist dropped from the parallel, is no idolatrous king of Judah but simply the fourth son of Micah, a link in Jonathan’s chain; and Gill’s care to restore him, with Ellicott’s note on the interchangeable Tarea and Tahrea, shows the commentators guarding even the least of these names against loss, that the house of Saul might be traced whole to its end. Ellicott hears in the pair Tarea and Tahrea nothing more than one name in a softer and a harder pronunciation, the kind of slight phonetic shift that passes harmlessly between the two copies of this genealogy. And Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the ten generations still to come, treat these sons of Micah as the first links of a single unbroken descent rather than a stray fragment of names. So the witnesses receive Pithon and Melech and Tarea and Ahaz as four more steps faithfully kept, and mark how tenderly the record recovers even a name that a scribe had let fall, that nothing of the posterity of Saul should slip into oblivion.
36:
The descent runs on: “Ahaz begat Jehoadah; and Jehoadah begat Alemeth, and Azmaveth, and Zimri; and Zimri begat Moza.” The reformed John Gill sets the name Jehoadah beside its variant, noting that he is called Jarah in 1 Chron. 9:42. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains how the two forms arose: the Hebrew here is Jeho’addah, and the Jarah of the parallel is a mistake springing from the common confusion of the Hebrew letters d and r, which are nearly alike in shape; the name there should really be read Jo’addah, a contraction of the fuller form found here. It is a textbook instance of the scribal slip the whole chapter has taught the reader to expect, one letter mistaken for its look-alike.

Ellicott adds a note on Alemeth, the first of Jehoadah’s sons, observing that the name appears elsewhere as a son of Becher in 1 Chron. 7:8 and as a Levitical town in 1 Chron. 6:60, though here it is plainly the name of a person. The witnesses let the verse carry the line quietly forward through three more generations, from Ahaz to Moza, each name a link and little more. Yet the very persistence is the point: the house of Saul, which might have ended in the lame Mephibosheth or been lost in the wreck of the monarchy, is shown continuing steadily, father to son, deep into the later history of the tribe. Gill’s pairing of Jehoadah with Jarah and Ellicott’s account of the d-and-r confusion keep the record accurate across its two copies, so that not even a mistaken letter is allowed to obscure the descent; and Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri stand as further proof that the Chronicler meant to trace Jonathan’s line, however obscure it had become, all the way to its close. The witnesses note too, with Ellicott, how these names hover between persons and places: Alemeth is elsewhere a Levitical town and Azmaveth a village of the returning exiles, yet here they are plainly the sons of Jehoadah, men and not localities. It is the same double life of Benjamite names that the chapter has shown from the first, a word serving now for a clan, now for a chief, now for the place he held. And beneath the philology the plain marvel holds, that the line of a rejected and long-dead king is followed here link by link, Ahaz to Moza, with a fidelity the Chronicler withheld from the whole idolatrous dynasty of the north, because to this house alone a covenant had once bound the throne that God would establish in David.
37:
“And Moza begat Binea: Rapha was his son, Eleasah his son, Azel his son.” The reformed John Gill offers his single characteristic gloss, that this Rapha is called Rephaiah in the parallel record at 1 Chron. 9:43 — one more of the paired variants by which the two copies of Saul’s genealogy differ in form while agreeing in substance. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, taking the whole remaining descent together, trace the succession here from Moza through Binea by way of the plain verb “begat,” and then, by the recurring formula “his son,” down through Rapha and Eleasah to Azel, of whom the next verses will number six sons.

The witnesses feel the change of texture in these closing generations. Where the earlier stretches of the chapter piled clan upon clan, here the line has narrowed to a single thread, each man named with his one son, the register slowing to a deliberate step-by-step descent as it approaches its end. Keil and Delitzsch’s attention to the connecting words — “begat” for one link, “his son” for the next — is not idle grammar but the tracing of an unbroken chain, and Gill’s note on Rephaiah keeps the chain tied to its parallel so that no link is lost between the two records. Azel, named last here, is the man whose numerous house will crown the genealogy, and the verse sets him in place as the goal toward which Moza and Binea and Eleasah have been leading. The house of Saul, so nearly extinguished, is shown reaching in Azel a late and vigorous flowering, and the Chronicler’s patient “his son... his son” carries the reader to the threshold of that final, fuller notice.

What little the witnesses can say beyond the names, they say with care. Each link in this closing stretch has its counterpart in the twin register of 1 Chron. 9:43, sometimes under a slightly altered form, as the Rapha here stands there as Rephaiah; and it is by laying the two lists together, name against name, that the commentators satisfy themselves the descent runs true. Gill’s single gloss is a specimen of the whole method, a quiet cross-check that guards one more link from doubt. The witnesses ask no more of so bare a verse than that it keep the chain sound and entire, and they find it does, handing the line safely on from Moza and Binea toward the household that will close the roll.
38:
“And Azel had six sons, whose names are these: Azrikam, Bocheru, and Ishmael, and Sheariah, and Obadiah, and Hanan.” The reformed John Gill counts them off, observing that these make up the stated number of six. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, however, questions whether the second entry is a name at all: some manuscripts, with the Septuagint and the Syriac, read not Bocheru but “his firstborn,” and this, he judges, is likely right, since the conjunctive particle that joins the other names is wanting before it, and Bocheru would be an anomalous form for a proper name. On that reading Azel had six sons of whom Azrikam was the firstborn, and the puzzling word is not a seventh name but a title.

Ellicott adds a note on the name Azel itself, that a place near Jerusalem was so called, mentioned by the prophets in Zech. 14:5 and Micah 1:11 — a reminder that in these registers the same word may name a man in one place and a locality in another. The witnesses read the verse as the fullest notice of any single household in the closing genealogy, the six sons of Azel set down by name where their forefathers had passed with barely a word apiece. Gill’s plain count and Ellicott’s careful weighing of Bocheru against “his firstborn” both serve the accuracy of the record, and the length of the list itself testifies to the vigour of Saul’s house in its latter days: a line that had run for generations through a single heir now broadens again into six brothers. It is toward this house, and the house of Azel’s brother Eshek beside it, that the whole descent from Jonathan has been tending; and these nine men, the six sons of Azel and the three of Eshek, are the last of Saul’s line to be named one by one before the register closes with the nameless multitude of Ulam’s archers. The witnesses feel the poignancy of the ending: the family of the rejected king, so nearly lost in a lame and landless heir, flourishes at the last in a crowd of brothers, in the very neighbourhood of Jerusalem where a place bore Azel’s own name; and the God who had taken the throne from Saul had not, for Jonathan’s sake, let Saul’s name perish from the earth.
39:
The register turns aside to a collateral branch: “the sons of Eshek his brother were Ulam his firstborn, Jehush the second, and Eliphelet the third.” The reformed John Gill takes Eshek for the brother of Azel, and confesses the man is otherwise unknown, unless he be the same as the Elasah conjectured elsewhere. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes his place in the line more precisely: Eshek is the brother of Azel and thus a son of Eleasah named at v.37, and the historian, having numbered Azel’s six sons, now develops the elder branch, the house of his brother, that the fuller posterity may be shown.

The witnesses note the deliberateness of this turn. The Chronicler might have closed with Azel, but he reaches back to Azel’s brother Eshek to follow his sons, and chiefly his firstborn Ulam, because it is through Ulam that the line will reach its most numerous and warlike flowering in the verse to come. Ellicott’s remark that “the elder line is first developed” catches the historian’s method: he pursues the branch that bears the most fruit, and Eshek’s house, with Ulam at its head, is that branch. Gill’s honest admission that Eshek is otherwise unknown, and his tentative link to Elasah, show the commentators reaching the far edge of what the record preserves, where names stand with little to anchor them but their place in the descent. Yet the line holds: Ulam, Jehush, and Eliphelet are set down in order, the firstborn marked out, and the genealogy of Saul’s house gathers itself for its final and most striking notice, the mighty men of valour who sprang from Ulam and closed the roll of Benjamin.

Keil and Delitzsch, who take the last generations at a single view, place Eshek’s three sons among the descendants they trace from Meribbaal down through ten links, and note that the subscription soon to follow, “all these are of the sons of Benjamin,” will fold this collateral branch, no less than the direct line of Azel, back into the tribe named at the chapter’s head. The witnesses find it fitting that the record should so carefully unfold a side-branch: Eshek is not among the heirs of Azel at all, yet because his firstborn Ulam would father the tribe’s last great company of warriors, the Chronicler turns aside to give his three sons their place. It is the same evenhandedness the chapter has shown throughout, numbering not only the houses that led but the houses that flourished, and refusing to let even a brother’s line fall out of the reckoning of Benjamin.
40:
The genealogy of Benjamin closes on a note of martial vigour: “the sons of Ulam were mighty men of valour, archers, and had many sons, and sons’ sons, an hundred and fifty. All these are of the sons of Benjamin.” The reformed John Gill notes that they were men of great fortitude and courage, though their individual names are not set down. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out the ancient reputation behind the words: the prowess of Benjamin was famed from of old, from the left-handed slingers of Judg. 20:16 and the deliverer Ehud of Judg. 3:15; and “archers” is literally “treaders of the bow,” as in 1 Chron. 5:18, for their bows were so great that the foot was set against them to bend them. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already seized on the number: of this one man’s posterity there were, as it seems, a hundred and fifty mighty archers brought into the field at once, and it is noted of them, more to their praise than pomp or wealth would be, that they were qualified to serve their country.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum’s pious gloss, that the sons of Ulam were mighty men who subdued their evil concupiscence by wisdom as men bend a bow, and therefore were blessed with many sons and grandsons; and he cannot forbear a jibe at the rabbinic excess, that Jarchi reckoned the allegorical expositions of Azel’s six sons enough to load thirteen thousand camels — a shameful hyperbole, he says, from an age when the metaphor-mongers ranked high, and may they never recover their footing. Ellicott closes with the historian’s question of date: if no names have dropped, these are the fourteenth generation from Jonathan, falling near the reign of Josiah; but the omission of a link or two, and the size of the family, incline him to think they really represent a house of the period of the Return, like the clans of comparable number that came up with Zerubbabel in Ezra 2:18, and he even hears in the Ishmael of Azel’s sons an echo of the Ishmael of the seed royal who survived the fall of Jerusalem in 2 Kings 25:25. The witnesses end the chapter where it began, at the whole tribe: the closing words “all these are of the sons of Benjamin” reach back over the entire roll to the superscription of v.1, and the tribe once cut down at Gibeah is left standing in its heirs, a hundred and fifty archers of the house of Saul, valiant to the last and fit to serve.
16:
The tally of Beriah’s sons is closed off with a naming formula: “Michael, and Ispah, and Joha, the sons of Beriah.” The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat this as the seal on the little list begun above, and use the moment to weigh a scholarly proposal about the names that come next. Bertheau, they note, would have three of the sons of Elpaal — Meshullam, Heber, and Ishmerai — be the same as Misham, Eber, and Shemer named earlier; but they will not have it, resting their refusal on the doubt whether the Elpaal of these verses is the Elpaal of v.12 at all. The reformed John Gill, whose gloss on the preceding verse already gathered these names as Beriah’s, needs add nothing but the connection.

The witnesses find in the closing tag “the sons of Beriah” a specimen of the register’s method: a cluster of names is given and then stamped with the father to whom they belong, so that the reader can always trace the branch even where the trunk was named some lines before. It is this careful labelling, Keil and Delitzsch imply, that lets the whole difficult passage be read at all, each knot of names anchored to its house. And their steady refusal to melt Meshullam and Heber into Misham and Eber is of a piece with the caution the chapter everywhere teaches: resemblance of names is not identity of persons, and a genealogy so full of recurring names must be read with a firm hand, keeping the branches distinct that the text keeps distinct. Michael and Ispah and Joha are thus left as the last-named sons of Beriah, a clan-head’s household duly registered and set in its place, before the roll turns to the descendants of Elpaal by another line. These three, Keil and Delitzsch imply, are found nowhere else in the whole of Scripture as this household is here recorded, so that the verse before us is their only monument; and the witnesses take the closing formula “the sons of Beriah” as the historian’s way of drawing a firm boundary round the little family, lest the names that follow be swept back into it. It is by such careful stops and headings, they observe, that a register otherwise all but drowned in recurring names is kept legible at all.
18:
With “Ishmerai also, and Jezliah, and Jobab, the sons of Elpaal,” the long roll of Elpaal’s descendants is brought to its close, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch stand back to survey the whole. From here the register will pass to the nine sons of Shimhi, the eleven sons of Shashak, and the six sons of Jeroham, and of all of them, they say frankly, nothing further is known, though some of the names are met with elsewhere singly. The concluding remark that comes at v.28, “these were heads of the fathers by their generations, chief men,” refers without doubt to the whole company, reaching back over every name from the sons of Beriah down to the sons of Jeroham. The reformed John Gill, whose connective on the preceding verse already gathered these last sons of Elpaal in, is content to let them stand.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as a hinge in the architecture of the list. It seals one great branch — the sons of Elpaal, spread through Ono and Lod and Aijalon — and prepares the transition to three further clans whose only memorial is their names. Keil and Delitzsch’s candid “nothing further is known” is not a shrug but a fidelity; they will not dress the silence in invented history, and they let the reader see exactly how much and how little the record yields. What it yields is enough for the Chronicler’s purpose: a roster of the chief houses of Benjamin, gathered under the heading that will crown them all, the heads of the fathers who dwelt in Jerusalem. Jobab and Ishmerai and Jezliah close the account of Elpaal, and the eye of the register lifts toward the summation that will bind this whole crowded passage into a single statement, that these were the chief men of their generations, settled in the holy city. Keil and Delitzsch add one last grammatical scruple of their own, that the words following in the summation, “according to their generations, heads,” stand in apposition to what precedes, though the precise force of the apposition they own to be doubtful — a candour of the same piece with their “nothing further is known,” unwilling to claim more certainty than the text affords. The witnesses thus close the long roll of Elpaal not with a flourish but with an honest reckoning of how much the record yields and how much it keeps back, and turn to gather the whole crowded passage under its single heading.
11:
The verse repeats and steadies what the last had begun, that of Hushim Shaharaim begat Abitub and Elpaal, and here the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott alone lingers to draw out its sense. These, he explains, were the offspring of Shaharaim by Hushim before her divorce — in other words, two offshoots of the clan of Shaharaim that settled in the neighbourhood of Lod, or Lydda, named at v.12, and that took no part in the emigration to Moab. The reformed John Gill’s companion note, that this pair was born before Shaharaim sent Hushim away, belongs equally here, marking the same division between the clans left behind and those carried across the Jordan.

The witnesses find the interest of so slight a verse in the geography it quietly opens. Where the sons of Hodesh spread through the land of Moab, the sons of Hushim stayed on the western side, settling toward Lod and Ono in the Benjamite lowland; the one branch went into exile and the other kept the old country. Ellicott’s reading, in the figurative idiom he has traced through the chapter, treats the “divorce” of Hushim as the parting of a clan from its migrating kin, so that Abitub and Elpaal represent the households that would not follow Shaharaim to Moab but held their ground near Lydda. The single verse, then, is a fork in the family’s road: from Elpaal, who stayed, will come the great spreading list of Benjamite clans that people Ono and Aijalon and Jerusalem, while the Moabite sons of Hodesh, once counted, drop from view. The Chronicler’s repetition is not idle; it fastens attention on the branch that mattered for the after-history of the tribe, the one that remained in the land and multiplied there.

The witnesses find a further point in the branch’s staying west of the Jordan. It is precisely these lowland clans, seated toward Lod and Ono, whose towns would be named again among the villages resettled after the exile; the households that clung to the land, and not those carried into Moab, are the ones whose seats the returning people would one day reclaim. So the Chronicler’s lingering over Abitub and Elpaal, and Ellicott’s care to mark them as the clans that took no part in the emigration, together point beyond the genealogy to its practical end, the guiding of the returned captives to the places their fathers had held. What looks like mere repetition is in truth the historian keeping his eye on the households whose homes the exiles would repossess.
31:
“And Gedor, and Ahio, and Zacher” complete the sons of Jehiel, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over two of the three names to sharpen the text. The recurrence of Ahio here, he notes, lends some support to the Authorised Version’s reading of the same name back at v.14, a small cross-check of one part of the register against another. And Zacher, he says, is really Zecher — the same softening seen in Shamer for Shemer — which is in fact the Zechariah of 1 Chron. 9:37, its fuller form; such abbreviations of names are common in these lists.

But the weightier point is what the verse has lost. After Zecher, Ellicott observes, the words “and Mikloth” have dropped out of the text, and their loss is easily explained, for the next verse begins with those very words, “and Mikloth begat Shimeah,” so that a scribe’s eye slipped from the first to the second — the more plainly since the parallel at 1 Chron. 9:33 preserves what is wanting here. The witnesses read the verse, then, as a quiet demonstration of how these genealogies were copied and where they suffered: a name softened, another abbreviated to its short form, and a whole clause fallen away by the recurrence of identical words. Yet nothing of substance is lost, for the missing Mikloth is at once recovered in the following line as the father of Shimeah. Ellicott’s patient comparison with the parallel register keeps the list whole, showing that the gaps of the one copy are filled by the other, and that the sons of Jehiel, Gedor and Ahio and Zecher and the recovered Mikloth, stand complete when the two records are read together.

Keil and Delitzsch, handling the sons of Jehiel as one group, confirm that these names belong with the household of Gibeon whose descendants the chapter is unfolding toward Saul, and that the whole list is to be read against its twin in the following book. The witnesses make of the verse a small lesson in how the two genealogies serve one another: what a scribe’s eye let fall from the one, the other preserves, so that neither copy alone is sufficient and neither is to be despised. Ellicott’s patient collation — restoring the lost “and Mikloth” from the parallel and recognizing Zecher as the fuller Zechariah — shows the very method by which these damaged registers are made whole again; and the sons of Jehiel, complete only when the records are joined, stand ready to yield in the next verses the direct ancestry of the first king of Israel.
20:
The house of Shimhi is carried on in “Elienai, and Zilthai, and Eliel,” three more of the nine sons whose only record is this line. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who gather the whole household together, can say of them what they said of the rest: nothing further is known, and though a name may surface elsewhere by itself, none of these men is met with again as a son of Shimhi. The reformed John Gill’s connective on the previous verse reaches over them too, enrolling them among “all the rest in these verses.” The register keeps them, and keeps them only as names.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the frame that gives such names their dignity, in his note on the coming summation: these are not individual members of single families but heads of clans, chiefs of the divisions of Benjamin, and the very repetition of the word “heads” at v.28 is meant to warn the reader against mistaking them for a bare family tree. Read so, Elienai and Zilthai and Eliel are not obscure individuals lost in a crowd but the standing-heads of Benjamite households, each name a company of kindred. The witnesses thus turn what could seem the driest fragment of the chapter into something weightier: a roll of the clans of a tribe, gathered toward the great summation that they dwelt at last in Jerusalem. And they hold to the discipline the whole passage has taught, receiving the names as the text gives them, neither padding the silence with invention nor slighting the men because their deeds are gone. To stand in this register at all is to be counted among the chief houses of the covenant people, and that, the chapter insists, is honour enough. Ellicott’s frame does further service here, for it sets these Jerusalem-dwelling clans over against the other branches of Benjamin scattered at Geba and Manahath, in Moab, at Lod and Ono and Aijalon; and Elienai, Zilthai, and Eliel belong, on his reading, to the households that would hold the holy city rather than the outlying towns. So even a fragment of three names carries a quiet geography, distinguishing the branch that settled where the temple stood from the kindred who dwelt abroad; and the witnesses receive the three as the text gives them, adding nothing and losing nothing, chiefs of the covenant people set in their appointed place.
